coredata.dc:title: Turn-on fluorescent assay based on purification system via magnetic separation for highly sensitive probing of adenosine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Wanying
Shen, Xin
Zhu, Chunhong
Li, Bingzhi
Hong, Junli
Zhou, Xuemin
coredata.dc:description: In this work, based on purifying the analysis system by magnetic separation, we constructed a turn-on fluorescent assay for highly sensitive detection of possible biomarkers, employing adenosine as the model target analyte. Aptamer labeled-carbon dots (Apt-CDs) was applied as fluorescence probes as well as selective recognition elements, while Fe3O4@polypyrrole (Fe3O4@PPY) was used as fluorescence quenching and magnetic separating materials for system purification. In absence of adenosine, the Apt-CDs were attached to the surface of PPY through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interaction and its fluorescence was quenched by Fe3O4@PPY. In presence of adenosine, the aptamer could specifically bind with adenosine to form a three dimensional Apt-CDs-adenosine complex and detach from PPY, leading to the fluorescence recovery of Apt-CDs. More importantly, with analysis system purification via twice convenient magnetic separations of free CDs and Apt-CDs-adenosine complex respectively, the detection limit could be reduced from 8 nmol L−1 to 0.15 nmol L−1. This concept offers potential application for simple, rapid, cost-effective, and highly sensitive assay of biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Turn-on fluorescent assay
Purification system
Magnetic separation
Aptamer labeled-carbon dots (Apt-CDs)
Fe3O4@polypyrrole (Fe3O4@PPY)
Adenosine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Advantage of the magnetism of Fe3O4@PPY

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Graduate Innovation Project of Jiangsu Province
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Graduate Research and Innovation Projects of Jiangsu Province

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81773681 , 81572081 , 81273480 , 21175070 ) and Graduate Innovation Project of Jiangsu Province ( No.KYZZ16_399 ). This work was also supported by the joint-training PhD program of Nanjing Medical University ( No.C004 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of assaying adenosine based on Apt-CDs and Fe3O4@PPY NPs by (a) direct detection and (b) detection after magnetic separation and purification.
TEM image (A) and FT-IR spectrum (B) of Fe3O4@PPY NPs, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption (Abs), excitation (EX) and emission (EM) spectra of CDs; photographs of prepared CDs under room light (inset, left) and under 365 nm UV lamp light (inset, right). (B) TEM image of CDs; Inset: HRTEM image and particle size distribution images of CDs.
XPS survey spectra of as-prepared CDs and Apt-CDs; XPS high resolution scan of the C1 s (inset).
Comparison of the fluorescence intensity recovery between direct detection and magnetic separation.

(A) Fluorescence intensity changes (F-F0)/F0 of the proposed assay to 100 nM adenosine, guanosine, uridine, cytidine, thymidine and mixture, respectively. (B) Fluorescence response of the proposed assay with magnetic separation in the presence of adenosine with increasing concentration; the inset: the calibration curve. (EX wavelength: 360 nm).
Result of detection of adenosine in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Turn-on fluorescent assay based on purification system via magnetic separation for highly sensitive probing of adenosine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, 211166, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, based on purifying the analysis system by magnetic separation, we constructed a turn-on fluorescent assay for highly sensitive detection of possible biomarkers, employing adenosine as the model target analyte. Aptamer labeled-carbon dots (Apt-CDs) was applied as fluorescence probes as well as selective recognition elements, while Fe3O4@polypyrrole (Fe3O4@PPY) was used as fluorescence quenching and magnetic separating materials for system purification. In absence of adenosine, the Apt-CDs were attached to the surface of PPY through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interaction and its fluorescence was quenched by Fe3O4@PPY. In presence of adenosine, the aptamer could specifically bind with adenosine to form a three dimensional Apt-CDs-adenosine complex and detach from PPY, leading to the fluorescence recovery of Apt-CDs. More importantly, with analysis system purification via twice convenient magnetic separations of free CDs and Apt-CDs-adenosine complex respectively, the detection limit could be reduced from 8 nmol L−1 to 0.15 nmol L−1. This concept offers potential application for simple, rapid, cost-effective, and highly sensitive assay of biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis has been intensively applied for the probing of different analytes in various reasearch fields such as clinical diagnosis, food safety and environmental monitoring due to their high sensitivity, high throughput and simple instrumentation [1–3]. Different fluorescence probes, e.g. fluorescence dyes, quantum dots and carbon dots (CDs) have been extensively utilized in fluorescence assays [4]. CDs with excellent optical properties have received extensive interests recently due to their exceptional advantages such as easy preparation, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and favorable water solubility [5–7] Aptamers, single-stranded oligonucleotides selected in vitro, exhibit high affinity and specific binding to large-scale molecular targets in biosensor applications [8–10]. Thereby, the combination of CDs with aptamer produces a fluorescent assay with high sensitivity and selectivity.

To further improve selectivity and sensitivity of bioanalytical methods and allow reduction of time-consuming analysis, the introduction of fluorescence quenching materials was exploited. Polypyrrole (PPY) is one of the most widely used conducting polymers in fabrication of bio-sensing field due to its low cost, easy synthesis and excellent environmental stability [11–13]. Ramanavicius et al. used PPY as an efficient fluorescence quencher in fluorescence detection based immunosensors firstly [14]. Recently, PPY was used as an acceptor for the first time and the CdS QD as the donor in fluorescence assay by Hashemian et al. [15].

Fluorescence assay was seriously interfered by matrix in analysis system (the mixture that was used to detect fluorescence intensity), so high purification of analysis system was required to enhance the sensitivity. Thereby, the separation of free donors and acceptors species was of great significance to obtain pure analysis system in the “off-on” inhibition assay. However, to date, the separation of free donors from inhibition systems to purify the analysis system has been rarely reported in fluorescence assay. In this study, Fe3O4@PPY was applied not only to quench fluorescence more efficiently but also to purify the analysis system conveniently. For the first separation, free donors CDs were removed, leading to sharp decrease of background signal. For the second separation, scattering interference from acceptors was declined significantly by the separation of Apt-CDs combined with the target adenosine from the system. Based on twice magnetic separation, analysis system was purified effectively, suggesting the potential for the improvement of intensity recovery and sensitivity of the fluorescence assay.

Adenosine plays a crucial role in multiple critical bioprocesses, and it is an essential component of many biological cofactors [16,17]. That is, adenosine could be considered as possible biomarkers for cancer, and also shows potential in early clinical diagnosis [15,18,19]. Thus, the great significance of monitoring adenosine at the preliminary stage of clinical diagnosis and treatment is self-evident. Herein, a facile and novel turn-on fluorescence assay based on CDs as a donor, aptamer as the recognition element and Fe3O4@PPY as an acceptor was fabricated for probing of adenosine. The introduction of target adenosine triggered the switch of the aptamer conformation and the detachment from the surface of PPY; thus, led to the fluorescence recovery of Apt-CDs quenched by Fe3O4@PPY. Meanwhile, the sensitivity could be further improved with the introduction of magnetic Fe3O4@PPY. The as-prepared fluorescence assay was applied to detect adenosine in biological samples, indicating its foreseeable prospect in the field of clinical diagnosis.

Adenosine, guanosine, uridine, cytidine, FeCl2·4H2O and pyrrole were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6H2O and 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ammonium persulfate was purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Adenosine aptamer (sequence: 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-AGA GAA CCT GGG GGA GTA TTG CGG AGG AAG GT-3′) was synthesized by Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Hitachi F-4600 (Japan) fluorescence instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted by a PHI Quantera II spectrometer (Japan) and samples were freeze-dried before measurement. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were recorded using JEM-2100 (HR) transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). FT-IR spectra were achieved on a TENSOR27 infrared scanner (Bruker, Germany). Fluorescence decay curves were recorded by a FM-4P-TCSPC transient fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, U.K.). The zeta potential was determined by Zs90 dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Malvern, U.K.). The synthesis of Apt-CDs was confirmed by gel electrophoresis in 12% Native-PAGE gel with 0.5 × TBE buffer at 80 V for 50 min, and gel image was photographed by 3500R Gel Image System (Tanon, China).

Fe3O4 nanoparticles (NPs) were synthesized according to our previous report [20]. 4.72 g FeCl3·6H2O and 1.72 g FeCl2·4H2O were dissolved in 80 mL of water with nitrogen flow under vigorous stirring for 15 min. Then, Fe3O4 NPs were formed by adding 10 mL aqueous ammonia (27%) dropwise and rising the temperature to 80 °C. After 30 min rapid stir, the obtained black product were achieved by magnetic separation, washed by ethanol, deionized water successively, then dried in vacuum. Subsequently, 20 mg Fe3O4 NPs were dispersed in 12 mL H2O, 30 μL pyrrole (99%) was added with stirring for 30 min. After that, 3 mL of 0.14 mol L−1 ammonium persulfate was dropped and incubated for 4 h at 0–5 °C. Subsequently, the Fe3O4@PPY NPs were obtained via magnetic separation and washed by ethanol, deionized water in sequence.

The CDs were prepared according to the procedure reported in the literature [21]. Briefly, by heating 2 g citric acid at 200 °C for 30 min with a heating mantle, the orange liquid was obtained. Under rapid stirring, the liquid was dropped into 100 mL NaOH solution (10 mg mL−1). Then the aqueous solution of CDs was obtained by using NaOH to adjust pH value to 7.0.

The Apt-CDs were prepared via well-established carbodiimide method. 190 μL of 20 g L−1 EDC in 10 mM PBS and 200 μL of CDs solution were mixed together and sonicated for 30 min. After the addition of 10 μL of 100 μM adenosine aptamer, the mixture was further incubated for 4 h at 25 °C. Afterwards, the obtained Apt-CDs were refrigerated overnight for future use.

For adenosine determination, 20 μL of Fe3O4@PPY (1 mg mL−1) was added into 20 μL of above-mentioned Apt-CDs solution and incubated for 20 min to quench the fluorescence, then the free CDs was removed and the Apt-CDs- Fe3O4@PPY complex was redissolved after three washes. Subsequently, 20 μL of adenosine with various concentrations was introduced into the mixture and shaken continuously using a spinning mixer for 30 min at room temperature. Finally, the mixture was diluted to 100 μL by deionized water for fluorescence measurements. High sensitivity could be achieved with low-quenched and high-recovered fluorescence. Hence, the relative fluorescence intensity [(F-F0)/F0] was calculated to evaluate the output signal, where F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensities of the proposed assay after magnetic separation with or without target adenosine, respectively (Scheme 1 ). The direct measurement without magnetic separation was also shown in Scheme 1.

Serum samples of healthy individuals were obtained from Nanjing Brain Hospital and stored frozen when not in use. Serum samples were centrifuged for 10 min at 12000 rpm to remove particulate matter and the supernatant was collected. The analysis process was conducted as detailed in Section 2.5.

The morphology and microstructure of Fe3O4@PPY was characterized by TEM images (Fig. 1 A). It is evident that Fe3O4 nanoparticles with a relatively uniform size and granular structure are intercalated into PPY polymer. The stability of Fe3O4@PPY was discussed in Section 1 of supplementary materials.

In the FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4@PPY (Fig. 1B), the peaks at 1545, 1456 cm−1 (the fundamental stretching vibration of pyrrole rings) and 1297 cm−1 (CN stretching vibrations) could be examined, [22] and the other peaks at 1190, 1040, 920 and 789 cm−1 were due to in-plane and out-of-plane CH and NH bending vibrations [23]. All these peaks indicated the presence of PPY in Fe3O4@PPY. The shifting and merging of the peak from 570 cm−1 (reported in bulk Fe3O4) to 610 cm−1 corresponding to Fe-O stretching vibration confirmed the presence of Fe3O4 as well as the strong combination of Fe3O4 with PPY, rather than blending of the two components [24].

The optical properties of CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 2 A). In the UV–vis spectrum (Abs), CDs exhibited a strong absorption at 365 nm owing to the excited-state energy trapping by the surface states, leading to strong emission [25]. In the fluorescence spectra, CDs have optimal excitation (EX) and emission (EM) wavelengths at 360 nm and 460 nm, respectively. Furthermore, the CDs solution emerged the distinct blue luminescence under UV irradiation whereas it was highly transparent under ambient light (Fig. 2A, inset). These indicated that the CDs possess good optical properties.

The shape and surface morphology of CDs were observed by TEM (Fig. 2B). It demonstrated that the CDs were spherical and had particle diameters of 1.2–3.4 nm with average diameter of 2.4 nm (shown in the inset of size distribution) with 133 particles counted, which was similar to previous report [26,27]. Inset in Fig. 2 B is the representative image of individual CDs, indicating high crystallinity of the CDs, with a lattice parameter of 0.18 nm in agreement with the [102] facet of graphitic carbon [28].

The elemental components of CDs and Apt-CDs were analyzed by XPS to prove the coupling reaction. Fig. 3 showed the presence of C and O elements in CDs and the co-existence of C, N, O and P in Apt-CDs, respectively. C1 s XPS spectra of the CDs (inset in Fig. 3) exhibited binding energy peaks at 284.3 eV (CC), 286.0 eV (CO) and 288.0 eV (CO), suggesting the existence of carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs [27]. The C 1 s spectra (inset in Fig. 3) of Apt-CDs could be deconvoluted into three peaks (284.3, 285.5 and 288.0 eV) which were ascribed to the CC, CN and CO species, respectively [29]. An additional peak at around 285.5 eV (CN) indicated that the amino groups modified aptamers were bonded to the carboxyl groups of CDs successfully.

The aptamer and CDs modified by aptamer were assessed by gel electrophoresis and showed that the Apt-CDs moved slower than the aptamer (Fig. S3). This was attributed to the raise of mass-to-charge ratio with modified CDs, suggesting that CDs were coupled with adenosine aptamer successfully. Moreover, the zeta potential of CDs is −2.16 mV, owing to the existence of −COOH groups on the surface of CDs [30]. After modified with aptamer, the zeta potential of Apt-CDs changed to −3.39 mV, which was ascribed to the negative charges of aptamer, further indicating that adenosine aptamer was successfully combined with CDs.

To explore the stability and feasibility of the Apt-CDs, the effect of the ion strength and prolonged excitation was investigated, respectively (Fig. S4). Fig. S4A indicated that the increasing concentration of NaCl can hardly affect the fluorescence intensity of Apt-CDs. Also, excellent photostability of the Apt-CDs was observed in Fig. S4B.

The design strategy of our proposed method is depicted in Scheme 1. The working principle of direct detection is well described by fluorescent detection (Fig. S5A). In absence of targets, Apt-CDs would be adsorbed on the surface of Fe3O4@PPY by hydrophobic action and π-π stacking interaction between the nucleobases and PPY. Fe3O4@PPY quenched the fluorescence of Apt-CDs (curve b) resulted from the efficient electronic transference. In presence of targets, the aptamer on the surface of Fe3O4@PPY could specifically combine with adenosine and be triggered to unfold structure to form the adenosine/aptamer complex, disturbing the interaction between the aptamers and PPY. Such interactions enabled the release of the Apt-CDs from Fe3O4@PPY, which recovered the fluorescence intensity of Apt-CDs significantly (curve c). Moreover, due to the superparamagnetism of Fe3O4@PPY, twice magnetic separations were conducted conveniently with the aim of purifying the analysis system. First, free donors CDs were removed to decrease background signal. Second, Apt-CDs combined with the target adenosine were separated from the system to reduce scattering interference from acceptors. This strategy improved the sensitivity obviously on account of the pure analysis system.

The time-resolved fluorescence spectra of Apt-CDs and Apt-CDs added with Fe3O4@PPY were measured to further understand the theory of fluorescence quenching (Fig. S5B). With addition of Fe3O4@PPY (triangle dots), the fluorescence lifetime remained constant with Apt-CDs (square dots), indicating that the fluorescence of Apt-CDs quenched by Fe3O4@PPY might be a static quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Advantage of the magnetism of Fe3O4@PPY
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the merits of the magnetism of Fe3O4@PPY, the fluorescence intensities were detected with and without magnetic separation and the relative fluorescence intensities [(F-F0)/F0] and [(F’-F0’)/F0’] were calculated, respectively. With magnetic separation, the sensitive change [(F-F0)/F0] increased obviously compared to direct detection (Fig. 4 ). The noticeable rise is ascribed to convenient separation of Fe3O4@PPY. First, the coexisted free CDs would be removed with magnetic separation, thus resulting in lower background signals (F0 value). Second, the Fe3O4@PPY could be separated easily when Apt-CDs were combined with the target adenosine, decreasing the absorption of the excitation and/or emission energy of donors and also scattering excitation and emission light. These suggested that the magnetic separation raises the sensitive recovery, declines scattering interference and background signal significantly, indicating the potential for the improvement of sensitivity.

Fe3O4@PPY was selected as the quencher due to the strong interaction between PPY and Apt-CDs, potent ability of fluorescence quench, good stability and water solubility as well as convenient magnetic separation of Fe3O4. So, the mass ratio between Fe3O4 and PPY played vital role in our sensing event, which significantly influenced the quenching and recovery of fluorescence intensity. The mass ratio of Fe3O4 and PPY was optimized at five ratios (Fig. S6A). When PPY increased, recovering efficiency increased first and then decreased. Thereby, the mass ratio 1: 1.5 was chosen when the relative fluorescence intensity [(F-F0)/F0] reached to maximum.

Besides, to better understand the effect of concentration of Fe3O4@PPY, the relative fluorescence intensity [(F’-F0’)/F0’] of direct detection was recorded (Fig. S6B) for that the effect of the concentration Fe3O4@PPY was weaken in detection after magnetic separation. When the concentration of Fe3O4@PPY increased, an apparent decline of the fluorescence intensity (F0′) was observed. However, with too much Fe3O4@PPY, the fluorescence recovery by the addition of adenosine was weakened. Therefore, 0.20 g L−1 was selected as the working concentration.

The effect of reaction time and temperature on fluorescence recovery by adenosine was investigated (Fig. S6C). The relative fluorescence intensity enhanced as the reaction developed and then tended to equilibrium after 30 min. And, the relative fluorescent intensity reached maximum when reaction temperature was 25 °C (Fig. S6D). Thus, 30 min and 25 °C were chosen as final reaction time and temperature for recovery by adenosine, respectively.

To evaluate the specificity of the proposed assay for adenosine detection, we further determined the relative fluorescence intensity of the assay under optimal working conditions with other nucleosides (e.g. guanosine, uridine, cytidine and thymidine) with equal concentrations (100 nM), respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the obvious relative fluorescence intensity was observed with the addition of adenosine while the fluorescence recoveries caused by the other nucleosides were slight. Also, the mixture of adenosine and these analogues with the same concentration showed identical relative fluorescence intensity towards adenosine solely. Moreover, the effect of some coexisting ions and amino acid (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Cl−, SO42−, cysteine and sarcosine) was investigated with their concentrations 10 times higher than that of adenosine. In Fig. S7, these ions did not interfere with the detection. These results demonstrated that the proposed method had a stronger specific binding with adenosine and could specifically recognize adenosine from its analogues, the coexisting substances and ions, permitting its application in the probing of adenosine in a complex matrix.

To explore the reproducibility of the assay, the experiments were performed using three different Fe3O4@PPY and Apt-CDs obtained from three separate synthesis reactions, respectively. The relative fluorescence intensity was detected with a RSD of 5.6%, indicating good reproducibility of the assay.

The fluorescence intensity of the assay binding adenosine with different concentrations was further assessed under the optimum conditions. As expected, both via direct detection (Fig. S8) and magnetic separation (Fig. 5B), the fluorescence intensity enhanced with the binding of increasing adenosine and the calibration curve between the concentration of adenosine and the relative fluorescence intensity was achieved, respectively. The calibration curve of direct assay could be described as (F’–F0′)/F0′ = 0.38 lg [C (nM)] − 0.33 (R = 0.998) in the range of 20–500 nM and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 8 nM. The more sensitive assay by magnetic separation displayed linear range of 0.5–500 nM and detection limit of 0.15 nM with the equation (F-F0)/F0 = 1.1 lg [C (nM)] + 0.56 (R = 0.996), which indicated that the magnetic separation of the donor and acceptor species was promising for the improvement of sensitivity as well as the decrease of detection limit. As shown in Table S1, our proposed method was compared with other reported fluorescent methods in determining adenosine [31–36], suggesting lower detection limit.

In an attempt to evaluate the proposed method for practical applications, the as-prepared method was applied to detect adenosine in human serum samples. The analytical results were shown in Table 1 . Moreover, the recovery test was performed by adding different concentrations of adenosine into the same volume of serum samples and the samples were prepared as described in section 2.6 and detected as detailed in section 2.5. It indicated that the recovery fractions were in the range of 93.2% – 103.1% with RSD lower than 5.6%. These results suggested that the proposed assay has the potential for practical application of adenosine measurement in real complex samples.

A novel turn-on fluorescent assay for probing of targets in real biological samples was fabricated, employing adenosine as the model target analyte, Apt-CDs as fluorescent probes, and Fe3O4@PPY as fluorescence quenching and system purifying materials. Twice convenient magnetic separations by magnetic Fe3O4@PPY make it possible to purify the analysis system, providing a high promise of the further improvement of sensitivity in fluorescence assay. Also, the proposed method showed immense universality for other clinical biomarkers by changing the specific aptamer sequence. We believe that this highly sensitive and selective assay is an improved fluorescence method for biomarkers detection, and a valuable tool with potential applications in clinical diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 81773681, 81572081, 81273480, 21175070) and Graduate Innovation Project of Jiangsu Province (No.KYZZ16_399). This work was also supported by the joint-training PhD program of Nanjing Medical University (No.C004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.12.147.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Wanying Zhu is a graduate student focusing on synthesis and application of nanomaterials as well as novel nanosensors for Ph.D. at Nanjing Medical University. She was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2013.
Xin Shen is a graduate student focusing on synthesis and application of carbon dots for M.S. at Nanjing Medical University. She was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2015.
Chunhong Zhu is a graduate student focusing on design and development of aptasensor for M.S. at Nanjing Medical University. She was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2016.

Bingzhi Li is a graduate student focusing on synthesis and application of metal nanoclusters for Ph.D. at Nanjing Medical University. He was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2014.

Junli Hong received her PhD from the School of Traditional Chinese Pharmacy at China Pharmaceutical University. Her current researches include the design and development of separation method for analyzing metabolites of active components in traditional Chinese medicine.

Xuemin Zhou is a professor at School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. She is currently conducting research in two areas: (1) synthesis of molecular imprinted polymer and its application in electrochemical sensor and (2) fluorescent nanomaterials application in bioanalysis.
